summary: Graham argues that large income gaps are a normal result of large differences in skill and productivity, just as in chess or writing, and that treating money-making as morally suspect stems from a childish “Daddy Model” of wealth and an outdated history of fortunes made by theft. In modern, technology-driven economies, he claims rising income variation often signals healthy wealth creation and higher overall prosperity, even as many material and social gaps shrink.
key_points: Wealth is created, not merely distributed; confusing wealth with money leads to zero-sum thinking.
Most modern fortunes come from creating value, not from theft or coercion, which historically dominated.
Technology amplifies productivity differences, increasing income variation while reducing many material gaps.
Market prices reflect what people want, so “underpaid” vs. “overpaid” is largely a value judgment about tastes.
Suppressing income variation tends to reduce incentives for wealth creation and makes societies poorer.
money_quotes: Wealth is the underlying stuff—the goods and services we buy.
You get paid by doing or making something people want, and those who make more money are often simply better at doing what people want.
Technology should increase the gap in income, but it seems to decrease other gaps.
I'd like to propose an alternative idea: that in a modern society, increasing variation in income is a sign of health.
You need rich people in your society not so much because in spending their money they create jobs, but because of what they have to do to get rich.
key_insight: In a modern economy, big income gaps often reflect concentrated skill and value creation, and may signal a healthier, more innovative society rather than injustice.
